title: The first set (almost) decides the match
question: Overall, and split by tour category and tournament level, how much does taking the first set shift a pair's probability of winning the match — and does that edge survive if the match reaches a third set?
method: analytics.momentum_table on the real 2026 feed — Wilson 95% CI on P(match winner == set-1 winner), overall (n=668) and grouped by tour category and level (two-sided binomial test vs 50%, p<0.001). Deciding-set reset checked directly on set1 vs set3 winners (n=153).
takeaway: Set-1 winners take the match 86% of the time (n=668, p<0.001), consistent across gender and level. But when a match does reach a decider, the set-1 winner wins set 3 only ~49% of the time — momentum resets in the third set.

title: Playing together is worth ~27 points of win rate
question: Does a pair's win rate rise with the number of matches they've played together, controlling only for how much experience they have as a pair (1-3, 4-8, 9-15, 16+ matches)?
method: analytics.pair_chemistry on the real 2026 feed — bucketed win rate by matches-together-so-far, Wilson 95% CI per bucket; chi-square test of independence across buckets, n=1526 (pair, match) rows, p<0.001.
takeaway: Pairs in their first 3 matches together win ~40% of matches; pairs with 16+ matches together win ~67% (p<0.001). Caveat: survivorship — unsuccessful pairs split before accumulating matches.

title: Rankings predict — but don't decide
question: When analytics.fit_win_model predicts a given chance a team wins, do teams like that actually win about that often — and how far does official ranking alone get you?
method: Logistic regression of match outcome on log(team1_points/team2_points) over the real 2026 feed, n=763 finished matches, AUC=0.88; favorite (higher combined ranking points) win rate 78.5% (two-sided binomial test vs 50%, p<0.001). analytics.fit_win_model's decile calibration (ranking_gap / height_gap / surface_indoor) is shown in-app on the same real data.
takeaway: The team with more combined ranking points wins 78.5% of matches (AUC 0.88). Strong signal, far from destiny. Caveat: points are an end-of-window snapshot, not match-date values.

title: What Elo sees that the ranking doesn't
question: Which players' match-derived Elo rating (from analytics.compute_elo) is most out of step with their official ranking points — who is over- or under-rated by the official system?
method: analytics.compute_elo (K=32, base=1500) run chronologically over all 776 real 2026 matches, joined to pro_players_real.csv official points by player id (all 279 match participants matched). Restricted to players with >=10 matches (122 of 279) to avoid small-sample Elo noise, then compared each player's Elo-based rank position to their official-points rank position.
takeaway: An Elo rating built purely from 2026 results broadly tracks official points, but flags over-performers the ranking underrates: Juan Ignacio De Pascual (Elo rank #35 vs points rank #103, n=16 matches), Alonso Rodriguez Martinez (Elo rank #35 vs points rank #101, n=16 matches), and Ramiro Valenzuela (Elo rank #58 vs points rank #121, n=10 matches).

title: Upsets are rarer than they feel
question: Among seeded matches, how often does the worse-seeded team actually win — and does that rate vary by round?
method: Winner's numeric seed > loser's numeric seed, both teams numerically seeded (WC/Q/LL/LLI/blank excluded) — n=165 of 763 finished matches. Two-sided binomial test vs 50%, p<0.001. Wilson 95% CI per round.
takeaway: Only 22.4% of seeded matches end in an upset (p<0.001 vs 50%), and the rate is far from uniform by round: it's lowest in the semifinals (14%, n=43) and the Round of 16 (16%, n=31), roughly doubles in the quarterfinals (21%, n=67), and jumps to a coin flip in the final (50%, n=24) — finals are where the seeding gap matters least, not most.

title: Winning fast pays forward
question: Does spending fewer games winning your previous round give you an edge in the next match — an on-court efficiency or fatigue signal?
method: Two-sided binomial test vs 50% on whether the team that played fewer total games in its previous round (same tournament) wins the next match; n=233 team-observations with a known previous-round game count, p=0.004. Wilson 95% CI per bar.
takeaway: The team that spent fewer total games in its previous round wins 59.7% of the next match (n=233, p=0.004) — an on-court efficiency/fatigue signal.

title: No home advantage — an honest null
question: Do players competing in their home country win more often — or is 'home advantage' in padel a myth?
method: Home = at least one player's nationality equals the tournament host country; matches where both or neither team was home are excluded (654 of 763). n=109 matches with exactly one home team, two-sided binomial test vs 50%, p=0.18.
takeaway: Teams playing in their home country won only 43% of home-vs-away matches (n=109, p=0.18, not significant). We report this null openly: trustworthy analysis includes what the data does NOT show.
